topic: Session: Refactor validation package to pure functions
oneSentenceAnswer: Replaced a class-based Validator with three pure functions, removing 80 lines of boilerplate while keeping all 9 tests green.
fiveMinutePath: See what changed: 3 files touched, net -80 lines.
Understand why: the class was hiding state that didn't need to exist.
Review the new API: validateContent(), scoreContent(), formatReport().
Note the test strategy: same 9 assertions, zero test changes needed.
Learn the pattern: pure functions are easier to test and tree-shake.
whyItMatters: Pure functions eliminate hidden state bugs — the old class had a mutable errors array that leaked between calls.
Tree-shaking now works: callers that only need scoreContent() don't bundle the formatter.
The refactor is a template for two other packages that still use the same class pattern.

keyIdeas.title: Pure functions, no class
keyIdeas.body: validateContent(json) returns a plain ValidationResult object. No constructor, no this, no mutable state. The result is serialisable.

keyIdeas.title: Named exports enable tree-shaking
keyIdeas.body: Bundlers can now drop formatReport from builds that only call validateContent. The old default-class export prevented this optimisation.

keyIdeas.title: Zero test changes — the best kind of refactor
keyIdeas.body: All 9 tests passed with identical assertions. Behaviour-preserving refactors should not require test rewrites; if they do, the API changed, not just the implementation.

keyIdeas.title: The mutable errors array was a latent bug
keyIdeas.body: The class accumulated errors in this.errors across calls. A caller who ran validate() twice on the same instance got doubled errors on the second pass. Pure functions make this impossible.

diagrams.alt: Before: a Validator class with mutable this.errors. After: three named pure function exports.
diagrams.code:
graph LR
  subgraph Before
    A[new Validator\(\)] --> B[this.errors = \[\]]
    B --> C[validate\(json\)]
    C --> D[this.errors.push\(...\)]
  end
  subgraph After
    E[validateContent\(json\)] --> F[return ValidationResult]
    G[scoreContent\(json\)] --> H[return number]
    I[formatReport\(result\)] --> J[return string]
  end

cells.Pure functions (after): No state — result is a return value
Works — named exports are independently droppable

examples.title: Calling the new API
examples.body: import { validateContent } from '@recap-studio/validation'; const result = validateContent(json); if (!result.valid) console.error(result.errors);

examples.title: Tree-shaking in action
examples.body: A CI script that only calls scoreContent() now ships 40% less code because formatReport() is not imported and gets dropped by the bundler.

analogies.setup: A class with mutable state is like a vending machine that remembers your last order.
analogies.takeaway: If the machine remembers the wrong order, every new customer gets the wrong snack. A pure function is a machine that resets after every use — input in, output out, no memory.

analogies.setup: Pure functions are like a calculator with no memory button.
analogies.takeaway: Every calculation starts fresh. You can run 9 calculations in a row and the tenth is not affected by any of them.

misconceptions.myth: Classes are always wrong in TypeScript.
misconceptions.truth: Classes are fine when state genuinely belongs to an instance (e.g. a database connection pool). Validators and scorers have no per-instance state, so they should be functions.

misconceptions.myth: Any API change requires test rewrites.
misconceptions.truth: If the tests only assert on outputs (not on class internals), a behaviour-preserving refactor leaves tests untouched. Good tests are a safety net, not a mirror of implementation details.

glossary.definition: A function whose output depends only on its inputs and has no side effects — no shared state, no I/O.
A bundler optimisation that removes unused exports from the final JavaScript bundle.
An export identified by name (export function foo). Bundlers can tree-shake named exports; default class exports are harder to shake.
Data that can be changed after creation — a source of latent bugs when the same object is reused across calls.

practicalTakeaways: Check for mutable class state that could leak between calls before shipping a validation utility.
Named exports plus pure functions give you free tree-shaking in any modern bundler.
A refactor that requires zero test changes is a signal the abstraction boundary was correct from the start.
Run the full test suite immediately after removing a class — if tests still pass with zero changes, you refactored cleanly.

sourceMap.title: Git diff — refactor/validation-pure-functions
sourceMap.summary: The pull-request diff that introduced the pure-function refactor of the validation package.
claims: All 9 tests green with zero test modifications

uncertaintyNotes: The 40% bundle-size reduction for tree-shaking is illustrative; actual savings depend on the consuming application's import graph.
